summary: Gruber reviews Apple's September 2015 event, which consolidated all fall product announcements into a single 2-hour-20-minute presentation covering iPhone 6S, iPad Pro, Apple Watch updates, and the new Apple TV. He notes the Mac was entirely absent from stage time, but praises the event's tight structure and flow as a welcome correction from the rambling WWDC keynote earlier that year. He reflects on the challenge of replacing Steve Jobs as keynote auteur and finds this event demonstrated Apple can still produce a cohesive show. Among the products, he's most enthusiastic about Apple TV as potentially Apple's most disruptive product since the iPhone, and sees the iPad Pro's A9X chip as an inflection point where ARM may surpass Intel's x86 for portable hardware.
key_points: Apple consolidated all fall announcements into one event, sacrificing Mac stage time entirely — a format Gruber predicts will become the new normal

The event's stagecraft was a significant improvement over WWDC 2015, which Gruber calls the worst Apple event in the modern era, addressing concerns about whether Apple could produce cohesive keynotes without Jobs
The Apple Watch Hermès partnership signals a new model for luxury brand collaborations, with custom watch faces and co-branded products
iPad Pro's A9X chip is described as genuinely 'desktop class,' with Gruber predicting it could match a 2013 MacBook Air and potentially surpass Intel's Surface Pro 3
3D Touch is framed as the multi-touch equivalent of keyboard shortcuts — power user shortcuts that don't complicate the base experience
money_quotes: It's often painfully obvious that the public presentations of big companies are dictated by internal politics more than showmanship.
It was Jobs the director, writer, and editor who I worried Apple would miss. The keynote auteur.
I think it's a good example, though, of a product matrix that is complex but not complicated. People know what they like when they see it.
Let's face it, the vast majority of 'laptops' are piece of crap PCs.
It's the multi-touch equivalent of keyboard shortcuts on the desktop: shortcuts for tasks that can all be accomplished without it. To use the old parlance, 3D Touch is for power users.
I think Apple TV might be the most disruptive product from Apple since the iPhone.
It sounds like a small talented team got to build the Apple TV they wanted to see and use themselves. There is a clarity and vision to the entirety of its design.
The 'M' and 'H' in LVMH are Moët and Hennessy — champagne and cognac. That's conglomeration.
key_insight: Apple proved it can still produce a cohesive, well-directed product event without Steve Jobs, and the breadth of announcements — from Apple Watch luxury partnerships to iPad Pro's desktop-class silicon to Apple TV's reimagined interface — signals a company firing on all cylinders across multiple product lines.
